title: Language Models Are Greedy Reasoners: A Systematic Formal Analysis of Chain-of-Thought
authors: Abulhair Saparov, He He (Center for Data Science, New York University)
venue: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023 (arXiv v4, 2 Mar 2023; original submission arXiv v1, 3 Oct 2022)
claim: The paper introduces PrOntoQA, a synthetic QA dataset generated from symbolic ontologies and modus-ponens proofs, whose chains-of-thought can be semantically parsed back into formal proof steps for direct, step-level evaluation rather than label-only evaluation. Using this tool on InstructGPT/GPT-3, the authors claim LLMs (specifically the largest model, text-davinci-002) are capable of producing valid individual deduction steps, even in fictional contexts, but have difficulty with 'proof planning': when multiple valid next steps are available at a branch point, the model frequently selects a misleading one and cannot systematically recover.
novelty: The authors state explicitly that while the dataset itself is an important contribution, 'the main contribution is the analysis that is facilitated by the dataset' (Introduction). The novelty vs. prior work (per Section 2 and Table 1) is that PrOntoQA is the first dataset in this comparison set to simultaneously: (1) provide easily semantically-parseable proofs/CoT (unlike FOLIO, which evaluates only predicted labels), (2) control for true vs. false vs. fictional ontology context, (3) control for unary vs. binary predicates, (4) control for the specific deduction rules used, (5) test reasoning beyond math word problems, and (6) release generation code. This lets them decompose 'proof accuracy' into fine-grained categories (strictly-valid/broadly-valid/invalid; atomic/non-atomic; correct/misleading) that no compared dataset (GSM8K, ProofWriter, FOLIO, SimpleLogic) supports simultaneously (Table 1, Section 2).

protocol.description: 8-shot in-context learning with greedy decoding: each model call receives a fixed prompt of 8 fully-labeled example questions (context+query+CoT+label) followed by one held-out test question with the CoT and label stripped, and the model must generate the CoT and label in a single forward pass/completion (no interactive feedback loop within an instance). A separate self-consistency variant samples 40 completions per instance at temperature 0.7 and picks the highest-average-log-probability answer among semantically-agreeing samples — still single-shot per sample, aggregated post hoc, not an interactive/multi-turn protocol.
protocol.observation_content: Per the paper's own example (Figure 1 / Section 3): the model observes a 'context' paragraph of general rules (e.g., 'Each cat is a carnivore. Every carnivore is not herbivorous. Carnivores are mammals... Fae is a cat.'), then a 'query' ('True or false: Fae is not herbivorous.'), and must produce the full chain-of-thought plus label in one completion: 'Fae is a cat. Cats are carnivores. Fae is a carnivore. Every carnivore is not herbivorous. Fae is not herbivorous. True.' The model sees no gold CoT for the test item, only 8 preceding fully-worked demonstrations (context+query+CoT+label) as few-shot exemplars (Section 5.1: 'each input to the LLM consists of 8 fully-labeled questions followed by a single test question with missing CoT and label'). No partial/turn-by-turn feedback is given during generation; the entire proof is emitted in one pass and then parsed/scored offline by the authors' algorithm (Appendix A.4, Algorithm 1).

variants_compared: Ontology type: fictional (nonce concepts, e.g. 'wumpus'), 'true' (real concepts, real-world consistent), 'false' (real concepts, randomly/incorrectly structured)
Ontology traversal direction used to order context sentences: top-down (preorder, reversed relative to gold proof order) vs. bottom-up (postorder, matches gold proof order)
Predicate arity: unary vs. binary (named as a controlled variable in Section 2, not broken out numerically in the extracted results)

Prompting/decoding strategy: standard greedy CoT vs. self-consistency (40 samples, temp 0.7) vs. depth-first-search-style demonstrations (in-context examples containing misleading steps and recoveries)
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Real-world ('true') ontology yields much higher accuracy than fictional or 'false' ontologies, and its accuracy does not degrade from 3 to 5 hops, because the model can use pretrained world knowledge to 'skip' hops (Section 5.3, 'Real-world knowledge helps reasoning'). Fictional and false ontologies perform comparably (false slightly worse). Top-down traversal (reversed relative to gold proof) is harder than bottom-up, and this gap widens with more hops (Section 5.3, 'Traversal direction affects reasoning'); 5-hop top-down fictional examples fall to chance. Self-consistency (0.56 valid proof accuracy) and DFS-style demonstrations (0.55) were NOT significantly different from the greedy CoT baseline (0.545) in the 5-hop fictional top-down setting (Appendix A.7.1, A.7.2), i.e. neither more sophisticated decoding nor more sophisticated demonstrations fixed the proof-planning failure.

models.name: text-davinci-002 ("InstructGPT")
key_results: Only text-davinci-002 performs better than chance across model-size sweep (Section 5.3, 'Only the largest model is able to reason'; Figure 6)
5-hop fictional-ontology proof steps: 93.2% strictly-valid, 2.4% broadly-valid, 5.9% invalid; 2.4% non-atomic (Section 5.3, item list under 'How do LLMs reason step-by-step?')
Accuracy handles 1- and 3-hop examples well but falls to chance on 5-hop top-down fictional examples (Section 5.3, 'Longer proofs are still challenging'; Figure 4)
Self-consistency: 0.56 valid proof accuracy vs. 0.545 baseline in 5-hop fictional top-down setting (n=100), not a significant difference (Appendix A.7.1)
DFS-demonstration prompting: 0.55 valid proof accuracy vs. 0.545 baseline, same setting, not significant (Appendix A.7.2)

models.name: GPT-3 family (text-ada-001, text-babbage-001, text-curie-001, davinci, text-davinci-001)
key_results: Proof accuracy increases considerably from 350M to 1.3B to 6.7B parameters, but none of these (nor text-davinci-001) reliably beats chance (Section 5.3; Figure 6)
davinci (base GPT-3) performs as well as or slightly better than text-davinci-001 (Section 5.3)
Smaller models' first non-canonical error is more often invalid or non-atomic steps; as model size grows this shifts toward misleading (but still valid) steps (Section 5.4; Figure 5)

headline_numbers: 5-hop fictional-ontology proof steps: 93.2% strictly-valid, 2.4% broadly-valid, 5.9% invalid (Section 5.3)
Self-consistency valid proof accuracy: 0.56 vs. greedy baseline 0.545, 5-hop fictional top-down, n=100 (Appendix A.7.1)
DFS-demo valid proof accuracy: 0.55 vs. greedy baseline 0.545, same setting, n=100 (Appendix A.7.2)
400 examples per experiment condition, 48 experiments total, 95% Wilson confidence intervals (Section 5.1)

Token-limit exclusions: 4 of 400 examples in the 3-hop fictional-ontology condition exceeded the 2049-token limit for all models except text-davinci-002 (effective n=396) (Figure 5 caption / Appendix A.5 caption)
failure_modes: The dominant failure mode is 'proof planning' failure, not step-invalidity: at branch points where multiple deduction steps are locally valid, the model (especially the best-performing text-davinci-002) frequently takes a 'strictly-valid atomic misleading step' — a step that is logically sound given the premises but does not lie on the path to the queried goal — and this is by far the most common type of first non-canonical error for the largest model (Section 5.4, Figure 5). Once off the gold path, the model has difficulty returning: 'the more time the model spends outside the correct proof path, the less likely it becomes to return to the correct proof' (Section 5.4, Figure 9 discussion). Smaller models instead more often produce invalid or non-atomic (skipped) steps as their first error, with misleading-but-valid steps becoming the dominant error type only as model size increases (Section 5.4). Self-consistency sampling actually assigns HIGHER overall sequence probability to incorrect (misleading) proofs than to the correct gold proof on examples the model gets wrong (Appendix A.7.1, Figure 10), meaning probability-based aggregation (and by extension beam search) will not fix proof planning. Real-world-knowledge leakage is also identified as a validity confound: performance is much higher on 'true' real-world ontologies, meaning label accuracy alone conflates retrieval with reasoning unless fictional/false ontologies are used (Section 5.3).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's entire evaluation is single-forward-pass CoT generation (8-shot demonstrations, then one greedy completion per test instance) — there is no interactive/multi-turn feedback loop tested. The authors do vary how much 'search-like' behavior is encoded IN the demonstrations (adding DFS-style traces showing recovery from misleading steps) and vary the number of samples drawn per instance (self-consistency, 40 samples), but both remain single-shot at generation time with post hoc aggregation, and neither improves accuracy over vanilla greedy CoT (0.55-0.56 vs. 0.545 valid proof accuracy; Appendix A.7). The paper's conclusion is that the failure is fundamentally in the model's inability to do global proof planning/search within a single generation, and that 'reasoning systems may benefit from more sophisticated proof planning/search strategies, such as neurosymbolic approaches' (Section 6) — i.e., the authors conclude more demonstrations or more samples in the current framework do not substitute for an actual multi-step search procedure; they speculate future work would need explicit external search/interaction, not just richer one-shot prompting.

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper is strictly single-shot (8-shot in-context, one generation per instance, greedy or sampled-and-aggregated) with no interactive/multi-turn evaluation loop. What is observed per call is the full context + query as one block; there is no partial feedback across turns. The output shape is a FULL CoT SEQUENCE (chain of natural-language sentences corresponding one-to-one with proof steps) plus a final True/False label, NOT a single label alone — the paper's whole methodological point is that they parse and grade the intermediate sequence, not just the label. The verifier is a symbolic proof-checker/semantic parser (Algorithm 1, Appendix A.4) that reconstructs each CoT sentence into a logical form and checks provability against the gold ontology/proof, categorizing each step as strictly-valid/broadly-valid/invalid and atomic/non-atomic and correct/misleading — a rich structural verifier, not mere string/exact-match on a label.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c18: Both use PrOntoQA's generative machinery: fictional ontologies with nonce concept names ('wumpus'/'yumpus'-style), controllable proof depth via hop count, modus-ponens (Hop rule) chaining, and distractor sentences to remove shortcut heuristics (Appendix A.2). c18's example instance and vocabulary (impus/vumpus, 'not loud') are directly drawn from this generator's fictional-ontology style. Both care about contamination resistance via fresh/fictional symbols and about depth as the core difficulty dial.
relation_to_candidate.differences: c18 scores only a single True/False/Unknown label via exact match and explicitly drops full proof-chain generation as output ('Full proof-chain generation as output — dropped from the design; the adaptation keeps the label only, to bound output tokens and preserve exact-match scoring', c18.md Dead branches). This paper's entire contribution is the opposite emphasis: it argues label accuracy alone is an inadequate/ambiguous measure of reasoning and builds a step-level parser specifically to grade the CoT, not just the label (Section 4, 5.2). This paper's PrOntoQA (as published) uses closed-world True/False only, with no explicit Unknown/open-world third label — the Unknown label and open-world semantics that c18 imports come from ProofWriter (Tafjord, Dalvi & Clark 2021), a separate paper, not from this one; the extraction of this paper contains no mention of an 'Unknown' label or open-world assumption (searched: not present in body text). This paper's protocol is exclusively single-shot CoT generation (no interactive loop, no repeated queries per theory) while c18's design as described also stays single-shot per instance (one query per theory), so on the single-shot vs. interactive axis the two are aligned; the key structural difference is output shape (full CoT sequence + step-level structural verifier here vs. single label + exact-match verifier in c18) and verifier sophistication (symbolic proof-reconstruction/parsing algorithm here vs. planned independent forward-chaining fixpoint oracle in c18, which the candidate notes is needed precisely because the T/F/Unknown label is otherwise 'definitional... not an independent theorem-prover verdict', c18.md Measurement skeptic).
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: c18 would add: (1) the open-world Unknown label and closed-world-assumption-vs-negation-as-failure distinction (imported from ProofWriter, not present in this paper); (2) a constraint-satisfaction/seating-puzzle stratum unrelated to this paper's ontology-chaining task; (3) an independent forward-chaining oracle to re-derive labels rather than trusting the generator's definitional negation flag, addressing a gap this paper does not need to address since it grades full proof chains rather than single labels; (4) explicit depth bins D0-D5 as a designed incremental ladder for a difficulty-controlled benchmark suite, whereas this paper uses hops (1/3/5) purely as an independent variable for its own step-level causal analysis rather than as a benchmark ladder deliverable.

corpus_claim: PrOntoQA (Saparov & He, ICLR 2023) is the fictional-ontology generator ('every wumpus is a yumpus') behind the codebase c18 plans to reuse, with contamination-resistant symbols and proof-step failure diagnosis.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper does confirm PrOntoQA is generated from symbolic ontologies with fictional concept names ('wumpus' etc., Section 3, Section 5.1: 'PrOntoQA generates examples with fictional concept names (e.g., "wumpus" instead of "cat," etc.)') used specifically to avoid pretraining-knowledge confounds ('to avoid any confounding effects from knowledge acquired during pretraining'), and it does provide fine-grained proof-step-level failure diagnosis (validity/atomicity/utility categorization, Section 4, Table 2, Algorithm 1) — supporting 'contamination-resistant symbols' and 'proof-step failure diagnosis'. However, the paper as written does NOT identify or discuss 'wumpus is a yumpus' as an example string in the extracted text (its own worked examples use cat/carnivore/mammal for the main figure and vumpus/numpus/dumpus/impus/wumpus/tumpus/zumpus/rompus/jompus in Appendix A.3/A.7.1 misprediction examples) — the exact 'every wumpus is a yumpus' phrasing in the corpus claim is a paraphrase/composite of the paper's actual fictional-lexicon style rather than a verbatim quote, which is a minor but real mismatch. More importantly, the codebase github.com/asaparov/prontoqa is confirmed by the paper itself (Introduction footnote and Reproducibility Statement) as the source of 'all analysis code, data, data generation scripts, and model outputs,' supporting the 'codebase c18 plans to reuse' portion.
baseline_relevant: Instance sizes/difficulty dials actually used in the paper: proof depth of 1, 3, or 5 hops; ontology type fictional/true/false; ontology traversal direction top-down vs. bottom-up; predicate arity unary vs. binary (Section 5.1). Ontology size ranges from 3 to 10 concepts (Section 3).
400 examples per experimental condition; 48 total experiments; 95% Wilson confidence intervals on Binomial(400,p) (Section 5.1).

Prompt format that swung accuracy: sentence ORDER (top-down/preorder vs. bottom-up/postorder traversal) mattered a lot — bottom-up matches gold proof order and is easier; top-down is reversed and harder, especially at 5 hops where accuracy falls to chance (Section 5.3).
Prompt format shots: 8-shot in-context learning, greedy decoding, OpenAI API (Section 5.1).

Distractor sentences are REQUIRED to prevent a trivial shortcut: without them, 'InstructGPT is able to predict the "true"/"false" label almost perfectly' by string-matching the queried property against context rather than reasoning (Appendix A.2) — directly relevant baseline-design warning for c18 to include distractors.

Metrics used: label accuracy, and four graded proof-accuracy metrics (strict, 'skip', broad, valid) of increasing permissiveness; the most permissive (valid) proof accuracy correlates best with label accuracy, suggesting label accuracy is itself a reasonable proxy for reasoning correctness IF distractors/shortcuts are controlled (Section 4 'Metrics', Section 5.2, Figure 3).

Floor/ceiling observed: only text-davinci-002 beats chance across the model-size sweep (350M to text-davinci-002); accuracy floor (chance) is hit specifically at 5-hop, top-down, fictional-ontology configuration (Section 5.3); real-world ('true') ontology gives a ceiling effect where accuracy does not degrade from 3 to 5 hops due to pretrained knowledge (Section 5.3).

Contamination/data-release status: all model outputs, generation code/scripts, and analysis code are released with a permissive open-source license at github.com/asaparov/prontoqa (Introduction footnote, Reproducibility Statement); experiments run via OpenAI API on specific dates in Sept/Oct/Nov 2022, so results reflect now-superseded OpenAI models (text-davinci-002, davinci, etc.), not current frontier models — relevant caveat for c18 since these baseline numbers are not representative of modern LLM performance.

Token-limit caveat: 4 of 400 examples in the 3-hop fictional condition exceeded a 2049-token limit for all but text-davinci-002 and were excluded (effective n=396) — relevant for sizing prompts/rules per instance at deeper hop counts (Figure 5 / A.5 captions).
quotes: "We present a new synthetic question-answering dataset called PrOntoQA, where each example is generated from a synthetic world model represented in first-order logic. This allows us to parse the generated chain-of-thought into symbolic proofs for formal analysis." (Abstract)

"LLMs are quite capable of making correct individual deduction steps, and so are generally capable of reasoning, even in fictional contexts. However, they have difficulty with proof planning: When multiple valid deduction steps are available, they are not able to systematically explore the different options." (Abstract)
"We emphasize here that while the dataset is an important contribution of this paper, the main contribution is the analysis that is facilitated by the dataset." (Section 1, Introduction)
"We use 8-shot in-context learning, so each input to the LLM consists of 8 fully-labeled questions followed by a single test question with missing CoT and label." (Section 5.1)

"In fact, we find that without any distractors, InstructGPT is able to predict the 'true'/'false' label almost perfectly. InstructGPT can utilize the heuristic that whether the queried property is mentioned in the context implies whether or not it is true." (Appendix A.2)

"Most predicted proof steps are strictly-valid (in the 5-hop experiments with fictional ontology, 93.2% of proof steps are strictly-valid, 2.4% are broadly-valid, and 5.9% are invalid)." (Section 5.3)

"Among incorrect proofs, strictly-valid atomic misleading steps appear in the proof first far more often than other non-canonical step types, including invalid steps. ... it seems that while LLMs are able to produce valid proof steps with high probability, they have difficulty with proof planning/strategizing." (Section 5.4)

"The resulting valid proof accuracy is 0.56 compared to 0.545 which is not significantly different. ... This suggests that our results do in fact generalize to more sophisticated prompting/decoding strategies, and that strategies that endeavor to find proofs with higher probability globally (e.g. beam search) will not help the model in proof planning." (Appendix A.7.1, self-consistency)
